Everyone there was a very bent pin found when Jose Vasquez & Kavin Ayala lubricated the chains, this was likely due to the chain crashing into the heating box again yesterday. Before any molds are added to the machine we must do a pin inspection for any. It doesn't have to be to the same scrutiny as we do for a mold crash as we are just looking for the very bent pin that was seen and any like them. Nathan Ruiz Nelson Cook

maintenance is still working on the C6 belt, and they are unsure of when that will be finished. Chad Stone I wanted to start depositing now but if the C6 belt is down it'll just sit.

What is happening with the C6 belt that they can’t start?

Is the C6 belt the demolding belt?
No the c6 belt is the one prior to the cavanna. It wasn’t tracking correctly and continuously drifted north. They got it now and we started depositing
We have to pull it to change it.
When starting it entire rows (going left to right) weren’t depositing. We manually deposit it does all of them. The operator believes it’s the wrong plates
Yes sorry they said blades and then realized it was plates
no prob!
But wouldn't the same effect those plates have on the dosing pattern be true for manual and auto?
does he use the same dosing distance in auto and manual?
That’s why I’m a little confused. Do you have any suggestions on why it would behave that way

The line is operating satisfactorily; both the temper and the screen are running without issues. Pre-piece and final piece weights are within range. Alan Todd Dave Ruff 
After working with Larry for a little while he did notice very quickly that 1. We were dropping product too late and 2. When the yamato was dosing it would have a delayed 3 to 4 pieces drop after the original dose which was the cause for pieces to get caught in the jaws. Those pieces were dropping late because the buckets were put on wrong. That picture is how to adjust the speed of when the product drops. We bumped it up from 700 to 900.
That adjustment is now set at 850
Alan Todd the choke point right now right now is the vibrators up top just not keeping up with the speed of the FFS. Until we figure out how to get them to run faster I think we are stuck at 48.
The over discharge was also on which allows the yamato to drop product even if it’s over weight so we turned it off.

We took our first test at 8:46PM:
40.9 degrees. 

The probe we used had a short end, so just the top layer was able to be tested. 

However, maintenance found their temp probe which has a much longer end. We’ll use that one on our next test at 11:00PM.
Did we take a temp when it was staged at 7pm?
No we did not due to the issues with not having a calibrated thermometer.

Just seeing this. I know I have a cert for the probe in maintenance. 
The plan for temp testing the heavy cream: 
* Pull a tote from the cooler, place it at the tote racks and take a temp reading. 
* Continue to take temp readings every 2 hours. 
* Post all findings. 
The idea is that the cream shouldn't go above 41F in a 4 hour period. 
Thank you for your help Dakota Adair & Conner Boyd !

Also, I checked with our kettle cooks and it appears that we only go through a tote of heavy whipping every 5-6 hours. 

Are we wanting to swap out current totes that have been out for more than 4 hours that are hooked up? 

So the tote we’re testing is under the same condition as all the heavy whipping cream that is going to line.
Ok. Thank you. We probably shouldn't have brought it out until we were ready with a thermometer. I appreciate the help!
No. Let's just continue the temp study for now.
